title: What is you-agent-factory
description: you-agent-factory is a CLI and agent-factory workflow system that keeps long-running agent work persistent.
openingSummary: you-agent-factory is a command-line interface (CLI) and agent-factory workflow system. It keeps long-running agent work moving across sessions instead of treating each run as a one-off chat.

whatItCovers.title: What It Covers
whatItCovers.body: you-agent-factory is software you install and run from a terminal. It organizes agent work into factory workflows so goals can continue over long periods of time. Customers use it to install the CLI, start named runs, and operate multi-agent workflows with shared harnesses and formats such as loops, reviews, planners, scheduled runs, and event streams. The product focus is persistent factory work, not a single disposable agent session.

keyConcepts.title: Key Concepts
keyConcepts.body: A factory is the workflow system that keeps agent work persistent and coordinated. A harness is the agent runtime the factory drives, such as a coding agent under a shared factory process. A named run, often expressed as a goal, is a concrete piece of work you start with the CLI so the factory can track and continue it. These ideas are enough to understand the product before you dig into install steps or system structure.

howToUse.title: How To Use
howToUse.body: Start with getting started to install the CLI and run a named goal. Use guides when you want a concrete workflow path such as loops or write-review flows. Open architecture of system when you need how the factory is structured internally. Product framing does not replace install docs or a full command reference.

limitsAndAssumptions.title: Limits And Assumptions
limitsAndAssumptions.body: Product framing stops at the overview. It is not a full architecture reference, component inventory, or runtime internals guide. you-agent-factory is not Model Atlas, an attention-reference sheet, a benchmark leaderboard, or a paper-download mirror. Readers who need install steps should use getting started; readers who need system structure should use architecture of system when that documentation is available.
